draft.introduction: In Aeneid VI, Anchises reveals to Aeneas the cosmic order of the underworld. At line 700ff, he explains the doctrine of the world-soul: a spirit within nourishes all things, and from this divine mind flow the souls of men, beasts, and birds.

translation.text: First, a spirit within sustains the sky, the earth, the watery plains, the shining globe of the moon, and the Titan sun and stars; and mind, pervading its members, stirs the whole mass and mingles with the great body. From this come the races of men and beasts, the lives of flying creatures, and the monsters that the ocean bears beneath its gleaming surface. Those souls have a fiery vigor and a heavenly origin.
translation.original_text: Principio caelum ac terras camposque liquentis lucentemque globum lunae Titaniaque astra spiritus intus alit, totamque infusa per artus mens agitat molem et magno se corpore miscet.

translator_notes.note: Anchises’s speech blends Platonic, Stoic, and Pythagorean elements into a distinctly Roman theology. The phrase spiritus intus alit (“a spirit within sustains”) became a key text for those who sought philosophical harmony between pagan theology and later Christian doctrine of the Holy Spirit.
